analysis.device: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition

bottleneck.description: Every one of the 20 Kernel2 (cuBLAS GEMM) dispatches shows smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active.avg.pct_of_peak_sustained_active = 0.0%, confirming complete Tensor Core disengagement across all matrix multiplications. The kernel name fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80 on all three SDPA instances contains an explicit 'f32' type tag, confirming FP32 data. On Blackwell (SM100), FP32 GEMMs that bypass the TF32/Tensor Core path execute on SIMT ALUs at roughly one-quarter the throughput of BF16 Tensor Core GEMMs. The Kernel2 register count of 210 per thread is characteristic of the FP32 SIMT cuBLAS code path (TF32/BF16 paths use 128-160 registers). Achieved occupancy is 16.6% across all mm operators — consistent with the register file being nearly fully consumed by a single block per SM (210 regs x 256 threads = 53,760 regs/block; Blackwell has 65,536 regs/SM => 1 block/SM max, which with 128 blocks on ~170 SMs leaves 42 SMs empty per wave). The fmha kernel additionally shows 757,760 local_memory_spills per launch, indicating register spilling under the sm80 compilation target running on SM100.

evidence.kernel: Kernel2 (cuBLAS FP32 SIMT path, representative k_00010 via torch_profiler high-confidence)

transformation.description: Cast all model weights and graph inputs to BF16. This forces cuBLAS to select the BF16 Tensor Core dispatch path (smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active will increase from 0% toward the roofline), reduces Kernel2 register pressure from 210 to approximately 128-160 registers/thread (increasing SM occupancy from ~16% to ~25-40%), and eliminates register spilling in the fmha kernel. On Blackwell, BF16 GEMM has 2-4x higher throughput than FP32 SIMT GEMM. After BF16 promotion, PyTorch 2.11's SDPA dispatcher should also switch from the xFormers memory-efficient path (fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80) to a BF16-compatible Flash Attention kernel that does not carry the sm80 compilation penalty.
transformation.location: All graph placeholder nodes (input tensors), all get_attr nodes for W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o linear weights, and layer norm weight/bias parameters. Output nodes receive a float32 cast if the caller requires FP32.
fx_steps: model = model.to(torch.bfloat16)  # cast all parameters before torch.compile; this covers W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o, LN weight, LN bias

Write a FX graph pass — iterate graph.nodes; for each node where node.op == 'placeholder': with graph.inserting_after(node): cast_node = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.to.dtype, args=(node, torch.bfloat16)); node.replace_all_uses_with(cast_node); cast_node.args = (node, torch.bfloat16)  # fix self-reference after replace_all_uses_with

Locate the output node: out_node = next(n for n in graph.nodes if n.op == 'output'). If the caller requires FP32, for each tensor in out_node.args[0]: with graph.inserting_before(out_node): fp32_node = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.to.dtype, args=(tensor_val, torch.float32)); then update out_node.args to reference fp32_node.
graph.eliminate_dead_code(); graph.lint(); gm.recompile()

bottleneck.description: The Q, K, V projections are dispatched as three separate aten::mm.default nodes in sequence, each with grid_dim [64, 1, 2] = 128 blocks. On a 170-SM device (GB202 Blackwell assumption), 128 blocks / 170 SMs = 0.75 blocks/SM: the GPU executes sub-one-wave, with 42 SMs receiving no work during each individual Q, K, or V GEMM. The three GEMMs are identical in shape ([4096, 512] x [512, 512]) and share the same input tensor (the layer norm output). Their sequential scheduling creates three consecutive GPU idle bubbles at the SM level. Observed durations are consistent at 59.5-60.9 us each (60.3 us average for block2 Q/K/V). A fused [4096, 512] x [512, 1536] GEMM would produce approximately 192 output tiles (3x the current 64), raising the block count to ~384 and achieving 2.3 waves on 170 SMs, reducing SM idle fraction from 25% to under 5%.

evidence.kernel: Kernel2 (three sequential launches at grid=64x1x2=128 blocks, one per Q/K/V projection per block)

transformation.description: Fuse Q, K, V weight matrices into a single parameter of shape [1536, 512] (in BF16, after OPT-1). Replace the three sequential aten::mm.default nodes that share the same first argument with a single aten::mm.default producing [4096, 1536], then recover q, k, v with aten::slice.Tensor. This is applied to each group of three Q/K/V mm nodes in the graph (one group per attention block). The output projection aten::mm (op_id=21, op_id=43) is not fused here as it depends on the attention output and is structurally separate.
transformation.location: Groups of three aten::mm.default nodes where args[0] is identical (the same layer-norm output node) and args[1] are distinct weight get_attr nodes each of shape [512, 512]. In the compiled FX graph these appear as consecutive nodes immediately following the aten::native_layer_norm or fused layer_norm Triton node.
fx_steps: Build input-to-mm-nodes map: from collections import defaultdict; by_input = defaultdict(list); [by_input[n.args[0]].append(n) for n in graph.nodes if n.op == 'call_function' and n.target is torch.ops.aten.mm.default]
Filter to groups of exactly 3 that share the same input and have weight nodes of shape [512, 512]: qkv_groups = [nodes for nodes in by_input.values() if len(nodes) == 3]
For each group (q_node, k_node, v_node): w_q = q_node.args[1]; w_k = k_node.args[1]; w_v = v_node.args[1]; inp = q_node.args[0]
with graph.inserting_before(q_node): w_qkv = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.cat.default, args=([w_q, w_k, w_v], 0))  # [1536, 512]
w_qkv_t = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.t.default, args=(w_qkv,))  # [512, 1536]
qkv_out = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.mm.default, args=(inp, w_qkv_t))  # [4096, 1536]

q_out = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.slice.Tensor, args=(qkv_out, 1, 0, 512)); k_out = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.slice.Tensor, args=(qkv_out, 1, 512, 1024)); v_out = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.slice.Tensor, args=(qkv_out, 1, 1024, 1536))
q_node.replace_all_uses_with(q_out); k_node.replace_all_uses_with(k_out); v_node.replace_all_uses_with(v_out)
graph.erase_node(v_node); graph.erase_node(k_node); graph.erase_node(q_node); graph.eliminate_dead_code(); graph.lint(); gm.recompile()
transformation.code_hint:
# Module-level equivalent (avoids FX surgery, triggers same Inductor codegen path):
# self.qkv = nn.Linear(512, 1536, bias=False)  # replaces three separate Linear(512,512)
# q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(512, dim=-1)

bottleneck.description: The SDPA dispatcher selected the xFormers memory-efficient backend, dispatching fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80. Three problems with this kernel on Blackwell: (1) The '_sm80' suffix confirms a CUTLASS binary compiled for compute capability 8.0 (Ampere A100), not 10.0 (Blackwell GB202). On SM100, this executes under forward-compatibility without exploiting Blackwell's WGMMA instruction set, the new ping-pong scheduler, or the larger shared memory. (2) local_memory_spills = 757,760 per launch: the 168-register-per-thread occupancy is at the sm80 occupancy cliff; a Blackwell-native FA3 kernel redesigned for SM100 register file topology would not spill. (3) achieved_occupancy = 14.04% (4 warps/block, block_dim 32x4x1), limiting the warp-level latency hiding that Flash Attention relies on. The Flash Attention backend (torch.backends.cuda.enable_flash_sdp=True) should dispatch a different kernel path that in PyTorch 2.11 + CUDA 12.8 may resolve to a Blackwell-aware implementation. This optimization is downstream of OPT-1: the Flash backend requires FP16 or BF16; the current FP32 dtype is why the dispatcher fell back to the memory-efficient path.

transformation.description: Enable the Flash Attention backend in the SDPA dispatcher before compilation. After BF16 promotion (OPT-1), the Flash backend becomes eligible (it requires FP16 or BF16). Setting enable_flash_sdp=True and enable_mem_efficient_sdp=False causes the SDPA operator to lower to aten::_scaled_dot_product_flash_attention.default instead of aten::_efficient_attention_forward.default in the Inductor FX graph. If aten::_efficient_attention_forward.default nodes still appear in the compiled graph (e.g., due to Inductor custom lowering rules), replace them explicitly with aten::scaled_dot_product_attention.default as shown in fx_steps, passing is_causal=False and scale=None to let the kernel compute the default 1/sqrt(head_dim) scaling.
transformation.location: All three aten::_efficient_attention_forward.default nodes (one per attention block). The predecessor reshape nodes (aten::_unsafe_view that rearrange [4096,8,64] -> [8,512,8,64] for BHSD layout) must be preserved as the Flash kernel requires [B,S,H,D] input layout.
fx_steps: Before torch.compile: torch.backends.cuda.enable_flash_sdp(True); torch.backends.cuda.enable_mem_efficient_sdp(False); torch.backends.cuda.enable_math_sdp(False)
Recompile the model with BF16 inputs (OPT-1 applied). Verify the dispatched kernel is no longer fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80 by checking ncu output or kernel names in torch.profiler.

If aten::_efficient_attention_forward.default persists in the graph, apply manual replacement: for node in list(graph.nodes): if node.target is torch.ops.aten._efficient_attention_forward.default: q, k, v = node.args[0], node.args[1], node.args[2]; with graph.inserting_before(node): sdpa = graph.call_function(torch.ops.aten.scaled_dot_product_attention.default, args=(q, k, v), kwargs={'attn_mask': None, 'dropout_p': 0.0, 'is_causal': False, 'scale': None})
node.replace_all_uses_with(sdpa); graph.erase_node(node)

Note: aten::scaled_dot_product_attention.default returns a single tensor (the attended output), whereas aten::_efficient_attention_forward.default returns a tuple. Downstream getitem nodes on the old output must be replaced with direct use of sdpa.

bottleneck.description: After QKV fusion (OPT-2), the FX graph contains an aten::t.default node that virtually transposes the fused weight w_qkv ([1536, 512] -> [512, 1536]) before passing it to aten::mm.default. cuBLAS receives this as an NT GEMM (A=no-transpose, B=transpose). Some cuBLAS tiling heuristics for BF16 Tensor Core path prefer the NN layout for the B matrix because the tile access pattern for B in NN mode is contiguous along the K dimension. Pre-storing the weight as [512, 1536] (i.e. the transpose stored contiguously) and issuing an NN GEMM eliminates the virtual-transpose overhead in cuBLAS kernel selection and removes the aten::t.default node from the graph entirely, also reducing the number of intermediate tensor descriptors cuBLAS must track. The gain is modest on modern cuBLAS but eliminates one graph node per attention block and ensures the optimal tile path is selected without relying on cuBLAS heuristic accuracy.

evidence.kernel: Kernel2 (post-OPT-1+OPT-2, projected fused QKV BF16 GEMM, grid approximately 192x1x2=384 blocks)

transformation.description: Register the pre-transposed QKV weight as a persistent buffer at model-load time. Replace the aten::t.default + aten::mm.default (NT layout) subgraph with a direct aten::mm.default (NN layout) using the pre-transposed buffer. The aten::cat.default node that builds w_qkv from [w_q, w_k, w_v] is eliminated by pre-computing the concatenation and transpose offline, reducing per-forward-pass graph operations by two nodes per attention block.
transformation.location: The aten::t.default node inserted by OPT-2 wrapping the aten::cat.default w_qkv node, and the aten::mm.default node consuming aten::t.default's output.
fx_steps: At model initialization, before torch.compile: w_qkv_T = torch.cat([W_q, W_k, W_v], dim=0).T.contiguous().to(torch.bfloat16)  # shape [512, 1536], contiguous row-major
Register as a buffer: self.register_buffer('w_qkv_T', w_qkv_T)  # avoids .parameters() iteration overhead

In the FX graph, locate the aten::t.default node (n_t) whose input is the aten::cat.default w_qkv node: n_t_nodes = [n for n in graph.nodes if n.target is torch.ops.aten.t.default and n.args[0].target is torch.ops.aten.cat.default]
For each n_t: with graph.inserting_before(n_t): attr_node = graph.get_attr('w_qkv_T')  # [512, 1536] pre-transposed buffer
n_t.replace_all_uses_with(attr_node); graph.erase_node(n_t)
The cat node (n_t.args[0]) is now dead — graph.eliminate_dead_code() will remove it along with the three underlying get_attr weight nodes
The mm node now computes mm(x, w_qkv_T) as an NN GEMM: x=[4096,512] x w_qkv_T=[512,1536] -> [4096,1536]. No further changes needed.
graph.lint(); gm.recompile()
transformation.code_hint:
# At model init:
self.register_buffer('w_qkv_T',
    torch.cat([self.W_q.weight, self.W_k.weight, self.W_v.weight], 0).T.contiguous().bfloat16())
# In forward:
qkv = x @ self.w_qkv_T  # NN GEMM, no virtual transpose

global_notes: EDGE CASE FLAG — ncu_replay_timing: All duration_ns values are 2-5x inflated by ncu application-mode replay. Absolute timing figures in this report are inflated proportionally; relative percentages and speedup ratios remain valid for decision-making.

EDGE CASE FLAG — fused_kernel_double_count: layer::unique::prologue_0 (is_fused=true, 16 kernels, k_00015-k_00029) and the individually named aten::mm / aten::_efficient_attention_forward operators (k_00030+) are attributed from DIFFERENT measurement iterations, not the same kernel launch counted twice. The prologue NVTX range spans two full attention-block passes; the individually labeled operators span a third pass. total_attributed_ns = 1,806,690 ns therefore represents approximately 3 measurement-iteration equivalents, not a single forward pass. The per-forward-pass wall time is approximately 1,806,690 / 3 = ~602,000 ns (~0.60 ms), consistent with the individual-operator sums for one block.

ARCHITECTURE ASSUMPTION: SM count for NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition assumed to be 170 (GB202 Blackwell die, matching the RTX 5090 consumer variant). If the Server Edition uses a different cut (e.g., 128 SMs), wave calculations change but the qualitative finding (sub-1-wave Q/K/V GEMMs) remains valid for any SM count >= 128.

DTYPE INFERENCE: The profile.json contains no explicit dtype field. float32 is inferred from: (a) fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80 — explicit 'f32' in the CUTLASS kernel name; (b) Kernel2 showing 210 registers/thread, consistent with the cuBLAS FP32 SIMT code path (TF32/BF16 TC paths use 128-160 registers); (c) smsp__pipe_tensor_cycles_active = 0.0 on all 20 mm kernel instances.

KERNEL ARCHITECTURE MISMATCH: The fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80 suffix '_sm80' indicates an Ampere (compute capability 8.0) compiled CUTLASS binary executing on Blackwell (SM100) under forward-compatibility. This means the kernel does not use Blackwell's WGMMA instructions, ping-pong async pipelines, or the increased shared memory capacity. After dtype promotion to BF16 (OPT-1) and enabling Flash SDP (OPT-3), PyTorch 2.11.0+cu128 should dispatch a different kernel. Exact kernel availability on Blackwell depends on the installed cuDNN/cuBLAS version; verify with ncu post-optimization.

OPT-2 SCOPE: The QKV fusion pattern targets the three mm nodes sharing the layer-norm output as their common first argument. The output projection mm (op_id=21, op_id=43) cannot be fused with Q/K/V because it takes the attention output, not the layer-norm output, as input. If the model has more than 3 attention blocks, the FX pass must handle all such groups; the pattern matching by shared input node is robust to arbitrary block counts.

OPT-3 CONFIDENCE NOTE: The availability of a Blackwell-native Flash Attention kernel in torch 2.11.0+cu128 is not guaranteed without empirical verification. If enable_flash_sdp=True still dispatches an sm80 kernel after BF16 promotion, the next step is to install flash-attn >= 2.6.0 separately (which ships Hopper/Blackwell CUDA kernels) and call F.scaled_dot_product_attention after torch.compile, or use the FlashAttention v3 Triton kernel directly.

ESTIMATED IMPACTS NOTE: Impact estimates assume 2.5x speedup for BF16 vs FP32 SIMT on Blackwell mm kernels (conservative; actual Blackwell TC throughput improvement for BF16 over FP32 SIMT can reach 4x). The fmha kernel impact (OPT-3) uses 1.5x conservative estimate because the current fmha already achieves 58.7% Tensor Core utilization in FP32; BF16 improves data throughput but the arithmetic improvement is partially offset by the kernel already being partially TC-bound. All estimated latency reductions are relative to inflated ncu-replay timings and should be scaled by the actual replay inflation factor (estimated 3x for this workload based on gpu__time_duration.sum vs duration_ns comparison).
